displayTitle: 2025-11-26 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill
topic: Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill
description: Thursday 27 November 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa Louisa Joblin, Member, Human Rights and Privacy Law Committee Neil Russ, Member, Tax Law Committee Financial Services Council of New Zealand Incorporated Kirk Hope, CEO FSC Mike Woodbury, Consultant, Chapman Tripp New Zealand Institute of Economic Research Peter Wilson, Associate, New Zealand Institute of Economic Research Julie Fry, Associate, New Zealand Institute of Economic Research Privacy Foundation NZ Katrine Evans, Chair Kathleen Ryan Yearly Meeting of Aotearoa NZ (Quakers) Marilyn Head Taxpayers' Union Jordan Williams, Executive Director Ray Deacon, Economist Taxi Limited Josh Taylor, Co-Founder Samuel Blackman Charlotte Evans Will Lau
descriptionSummary: Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill. New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa. Financial Services Council of New Zealand Incorporated

chunks.text: Good morning. Please, please proceed. It's also good to see, um, uh, Nui Walters here too, from Labor. So welcome. Thank you, Morina Coto. Call Louisa Jolin ua. My name is Louisa Jolin, and I'm a member of the Law Society's Human Rights and Privacy Committee.

With me this morning is Neil Russ, who's the convener of the Law Society's Tax Committee. Thank you very much for the invitation to come and speak to the committee this morning about the taxation bill. Um, if I may start by briefly outlining the Law Society's position, and then that will leave time for any questions that you have for us. Please, In all society's view is that the approved information sharing agreements or AA framework is the most appropriate information sharing mechanism in this context.

In particular, ACEs contain essential safeguards to ensure that personal information is appropriately protected. The process that set out in this bill amidst the usual important levels of oversight and transparency, and it's also dispensing with public consultation, which is vital for achieving objectives like transparency, public confidence, and external scrutiny.

The Law society is concerned about the precedent that's set by circumventing the AA process and with reference to the use of the phrase social license. In the explanatory note to the bill, the law society's view is that this language is problematic and the integrity of the tax system shouldn't be contingent on public tolerance for how much it can be weakened.

On the point of where the law society's view differs from the Ministry of Justice's Bill of Rights Vet, the ministry has not addressed Section 25 D of the Bill of Rights, which is the right not to be compelled to be a witness or to confess guilt. And that's closely connected with the privilege against self-incrimination that's set out in the Evidence Act.

Fundamentally, this is about ensuring that people are not required by the state to provide information which might expose them to criminal liability. The law society's view is that these issues might be triggered by the information sharing that's proposed in the belt and the proposition that information sharing is reasonably justified because it's supports enforcement of the law is circular in reasoning.

Lastly, the law society's view is that if the bill is to proceed with section 18 hb, then additional protection should be set out. These are included in our submission at paragraph, uh, 13.14, including requiring public consultation on an agreement before it's finalized, requiring a privacy impact assessment to be completed and consulted on together with a draft agreement and providing for review by the Privacy Commissioner of Agreements that have been implemented.

We'd be happy to answer any questions that you have for us on the Law society's behalf. Okay. Members, uh, s questions for Louisa. Can I also acknowledge, um, land Farm from the Green Party? Thank you for your presence. Uh, any questions? Okay, uh, Nui Walters, uh, thank you, Mr. Chair, And thank you very much for your submission.

Um, I do agree that there's potentially a problem in terms of the right against self-incrimination. I'm just wondering whether, um, the protections that you've suggested are sufficient in terms of addressing that. It would seem that that remains an issue. Um, I think part of my concern is the time at which information would be gathered as well.

So, um, if, for example, the police were to make a request after it's very clear to them that there appear to be some issues and the information is is handed over to them, um, that's one scenario. It's, uh, it's more problematic in my view if there's a conversation between police and IRD about, um, for want of a better term, phishing for information, um, that can then later be used.

And I just wonder if there's anything more explicit that we could, um, propose in terms of drawing down those powers of information gathering. Well, to answer the first part of your question, the additional safeguards that are proposed in the law society's submission are around the protection of personal information generally. So they're not specifically addressing that issue of self-incrimination.

Um, really it's the law society is quite generally concerned to make sure that there are adequate safeguards against self-incrimination in legislation generally, and that's a thing that is common in submissions made by the Law Society on similar, uh, similar sorts of proposals. So really we've raised it in the context of this bill because we've noted that the ministry has not addressed it.

I think if you would like the Law Society to consider that more specifically in terms of additional protections, perhaps that could be put back to the law society as a question that we can come back to you on in writing. Thank you. I if with the chair's permission, I think that would be useful. Yes. Yep. Happy for that. Please, if you can work with the, um, Clark's office with that.
Uh, Louisa, thank you. Oh, we've just frozen. Uh, Ms. Walters,

uh, was there a another supplementary question? Oui? Uh, no, that's me. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Okay. Any, anyone else? Uh, uh, in response to, um, the law Society's written or, uh, verbal, um, submission. Okay. And I see, uh, Neil, Russ there too. I wanna acknowledge you as well. So, um, thank you.

Thank you for being well with inside your, um, 10 minute organizational, uh, submission window, and we appreciate, uh, the effort that you've gone to. Thank you. Thank you for your time. Thank you. Okay, Mr. Uh, Mr. Um, Clark, we've got, um, Kirk Hope and others from the Financial Services Council of nz.
Uh, are they queued up for a 10 minute?
Yes, they're Mr. Chair, we just bring them in now. Thank you.

Oh, Mr. Hope, very good to see you. I know you've appeared in under a number of guises, uh, to the FEC over a number of years. Uh, good to see you with this hat on, of course, as, um, CEO of, um, FSC. So we're in your hands. Thank you for your submission. Brilliant. Thanks Mr. Chair. Um, I've got, joining me, Mike Woodbury from Chapman Trip, who's gonna cover off, um, some of the technical aspects of our submission around defined benefit schemes and, and, uh, and some QE service stuff.

Um, look, thanks very much for the opportunity. Um, I'll take, obviously our submission is, is read. Um, just to cover a little bit of, of the FSC, we've got a hundred over 110 members, man, managing over a hundred billion in funds, paying over 2.8 billion in, in claims annually. And our members include life, health, uh, life and health insurers, um, fund managers, uh, and KiwiSaver and Workplace Savings Schemes, uh, and professional services and technology providers.

So, uh, overall we are pretty supportive of the bill. Um, in particular, we've got some comments around the area of remedials for complying super funds, uh, and consolidating defined benefit schemes. Um, we obviously support the proposed changes to the foreign investment Fund rules. Um, there's a couple of areas we think that there might be, uh, some opportunity for improvement.

Uh, we are looking for further clarification and guidance from, uh, IR on, on the unclaimed money re remedials. Um, we would recommend an extension of the implementation date to allow industry readiness for that. Um, there is other significant regulatory change in the sector, and so these timeline changes need to be considered a bit.

Um, just one area that we focus on quite a lot in the bill, uh, that we feel could have gone. We, we, we feel the bill could have gone further on. That's in relation to fringe benefit tax. We've been working a lot in this space. Um, the amendments are pretty narrow in scope, and, and we think it's a missed opportunity to remove a key, a key tax impediment to enabling particularly, um, lower income employees to be able to access, uh, health and life insurance, uh, through through group, uh, employer schemes.

So FBT currently, um, currently operates is a significant barrier for, for employers to offer those to their employees as benefits. Um, so we think there's a real opportunity there and we would suggest that, um, new Zealand's a bit of an outlier in this space. Uh, many other OCD countries provide, uh, or enable, uh, the provision of, of these sorts of products through, uh, and, and incentivize 'em through tax concessions.

Um, this isn't particularly a tax concession. I mean, the, the, uh, previous government, uh, were, were pretty happy to provide, for example, uh, um, removal of FPT for public transport services, uh, for employers. So, you know, this is really about enabling people to remain productive and, and working and healthy.

So, uh, we think that's a real missed opportunity. Um, my other than that on, on this area, I might pass over to Mike now to, to give you a bit more of a, uh, technical feel for some of the components around defined benefit schemes. And, uh, so over to you, Mike, if you're there. Okay, Mr. Wood?

Yes, thanks, Mr. Chairman. Um, we warmly welcome the proposed Kiwi Saver Remedials, which I'll deal with first. As Kirk said, we'll take everything as read, but the essence of those is that in a workplace savings scheme, which also has a KiwiSaver integrated complying fund section with lock-ins to age 65, uh, members in the employer can continue with the current contribution streaming arrangements, whereby only the portions of the members and the employer's contributions, which each equal 3% of salary need to be locked in.

And the, in each case, considerable additional contributions can continue being paid in accordance with the somewhat more flexible workplace savings rules. There are about 14 or so remaining complying super funds. The the state sector dominated the biggest of unis, my super, which is the a CC one in the Defense Force Super scheme.

They are a grandfathered class of retirement scheme. No more can ever be created. And within them, the total balance is held on KiwiSaver equivalent locked in terms, or around 900 million for just over 12,000 members. But the aggregate balances are considerably higher. They're in the billions, and total membership is also several multiples higher.

Uh, they all feature aggregate employer contribution rates considerably higher than the KiwiSaver rate with few, and with few exceptions, that'll remain the case. Even if and when the, uh, KiwiSaver rates increase to say 6%, the average, the aggregate employer contribution rate would probably be in the order of about 10%.

So, um, these schemes exemplify good employer practice, what's been proposed, um, enables them to continue being key. We say we're integrated in a way which makes good common sense. Um, we want them to keep on keeping on because most also provide not only higher contribution rates, but also premium free or heavily discounted, um, group and TPD insurance cover, which, um, Kirk just touched on.

So that's a very welcome change. Um, we also welcome the agreed minor tweaks to the effect that if all or any of the, just under 50 or so remaining legacy pension schemes, workplace pension schemes in New Zealand, were were to amalgamate into a special purpose multi-employer master trust offered by a commercial consolidator, which would bring about much needed cost reduction and administrative simplification benefits, then that master trust would continue being taxed in the same way as those pension schemes are currently, uh,

the employer sponsors are in functional terms, life insurers. Uh, and but sensibly those schemes currently have an exemption from the life insurance tax regime. The issue is that the qualifying condition is the pension schemes must be single employer schemes. This obviously guards against a retail life insurer using the same model in substance.

The amendment will just say that the consolidation vehicle, if it consists solely of those merged pension schemes, will get the same tax treatment. We don't know whether a consolidation will actually happen. The schemes are only worth collectively a couple of billion or thereabouts. And overwhelmingly, they were closed to new joiners 35 plus years ago when tax exemptions fell away.

So they're a small sinking lid, but this amendment is an important enabler. So I'll leave it there and invite questions. Okay. Thank you Mr. Woodbury. Thank you, Mr. Hope I've got Dr. Deep. Deborah Russell. Uh, thank you. I just wanna, um, clarify something around the public transport FPT exemption that was to do with evening up the FPT exemption on car parks provided by employers.

So it wasn't a concession, it was just trying to even the playing field there in terms of the, um, FBT of health insurance. Um, have you costed that? Yeah, we've done, um, a reasonable amount of work, uh, and, and costing it. So the, the costs are relatively fiscally neutral for the government, and we've, we've provided some, um, material, uh, to ministers and, and to all political parties on it.

'cause it's, you know, it's something that really needs to be thought about across the house. Happy to, happy to, um, happy to pick that up and provide, um, further information. Of course, a large number of New Zealanders don't get access to private health insurance no matter what because of their employment. Uh, the alternative is just to fund the public system adequately so that, uh, private insurance isn't needed.

Have you considered that as an option? Well, I mean, I guess that's, that's the point really. For some people who are sitting on wait lists for considerable periods of time, they, and they can't access the public system. What we're saying is, you know, take, take some of that pressure off the public system, enable, um, particularly, uh, lower income, uh, workers to access health insurance at a lower cost through their employer, uh, group scheme.

So, uh, you know, that's, that's really the, the angle that we are coming at it from. But that money could just go straight into the public health system so that the waiting list didn't exist in the first place. There's a, there's a, there's a, a, a, a policy issue here as to whether we go private or public in the health system, and I'm not sure we should be doing it through, still through the FBT system.

Yeah, I mean, well that's, I guess that's for governments to consider. Really, uh, you know, what, what we're here to do is really, uh, ensure that, you know, new Zealanders in particular have, have access to, uh, the types of products and services that people have in other jurisdictions. All New Zealanders, or just the ones who are unemployment. Well, I mean, certainly the, um, the, the scope of employer based schemes is, is for those people in employment.

So I, this goes to the point point I'm making is you're making a fundamental change to the nature of the way that we collectively provide health in the system via a stealth system, uh, stealth move via FBT. It's not something I could support. Thank you. Okay. Any, anyone else members?

Not that I can see. We'll just do a quick peruse. Okay. Look, thank you Mr. Hope. Thank you Mr. Woodbury, for your, um, submission on behalf of FSC, uh, and all the best for the rest of the week. Thank You. Thanks. Thanks. Motion. Okay. Thank you. Okay. Uh, Mr. Clark, have we got N-Z-I-E-R? Uh, queued up?
We're running nice and early. Yeah, so just bring them in now. Thank you.

We've got, I, uh, here, Cameron. Well, I can't see them on screen. Um, Do you have a, Okay. Yeah. Uh, okay. Yeah, yeah. I know. I was wasn't, they weren't coming up on main screen. Okay. Okay. Thank you, Peter. Thank you, Julie. Thank you. Uh, Deborah. Um, so welcome. Just to give some context here, um, team, uh, as you would've seen in, uh, in, uh, the N Z's, um, submission, uh, this is exclusively concerned, uh, with the proposed amendments, uh, to the foreign investment, um, fund regime.

Uh, an interested group of parties has commissioned, uh, uh, N-Z-I-E-R to, to, um, put this submission together. So we, we await it with interest. Uh, thank you, uh, Mr. Chairman and, and members. Yes. We just wanna talk about the, um, the FEC, um, provisions, and we'll take it that you've read the submission.

So we won't go through it in detail. We'll just highlight the main points. Our summary points up, up front is that the, the current FIF regime is discouraging globally mobile talent from coming to New Zealand. That includes, uh, not just immigrants, but also returning Kiwis and Kiwis building innovative firms here.

Now, the bill is better than the status quo, but we think it doesn't go far enough. We think the focus should be very much on the, the impact of the fifth regime on high growth firms that are reducing weightless services, because that is a very exciting economic development opportunity for New Zealand.

And if we can grow that as well as the rest of the economy, then that would be a very good thing. We think that the modified fifth settings in the bill still discourage, um, people from coming to New Zealand, and therefore it should be seen as a, as a first step and not as a complete solution.

And we've outlined some amendments in, uh, in our submission that we'd recommend, uh, the committee, um, consider. Some of those are things that could go in the current bill. Others are things that we'd, we'd recommend that you encourage officials and the government to start work on, uh, as a matter of priority.

So that's our overall summary, and, and if you've got any questions about details, we can go through that. Right. Thank You, Mr. Wilson. We've got Dr. Deborah Russell. Um, thank you for the submission. Um, it's an interesting one 'cause I, I, I recognize the problem that's trying to be solved, and we do need to do something about the fifth rules. Just, um, and thank you for your suggestions around it.

Um, I was just, uh, one of the things is that this new RAM method won't apply to people who are already here. And you say that one of the motivations for these new rules is to encourage people to come here. So if people are already here, clearly they haven't been discouraged already, they are here, um, do you just, there's a little bit of a Yes.

Attention there. So how would you then justify applying it to people who are already here, even though we don't no longer need to attract them? Uh, it's to stop them leaving. Okay. Put, put simply, um, if, if, if they become, if they, uh, want to go and, and, uh, start a business overseas, uh, or want to stay here and start a business OO overseas, then the fifth rules will apply to them.

And, and that will discourage them from, from staying. And we've certainly met a, a number of people who that is the case. Do I was gonna say, do you have, um, evidence that that is actually happening? Yes, we have. We, we've talked in, in preparing this submission, and in our reports, we've talked to quite a number of, um, people who are in this situation impacted by the regime.

Julie Fry, my colleague lives in New York and has talked a lot of them there. We've talked to industry associations. This is, this is a material issue that is impacting on a number of, of people who want desperately to come and live in New Zealand or state. No, But I'm not talking about the people who want to come and live. I want to talk about the people who, so I recognize that point.

Yeah. If you've got evidence around people who are here and are leaving because of the fifth rules. Yeah. So, um, I spoke, I spoke with, um, 25 people for the initial report that we wrote, and two of those have already left. Right. Okay. Thank you. That's what I was looking forward just as a, just as an indication. Yeah. Yeah. Thank you. Okay.

We've got our deputy chair, Ryan Hamilton. Uh, thank you. Thanks for your submission. Just when you talk about the double taxation, and obviously this sort of targets American, um, internationals, but, um, why do you think the focus has just been on Americans? Was there another high sort of demographic that you think should have been included?

Uh, I, I think a lot of the focus is on Americans because of, of, of the US tech sector. Um, but I I, I would not like the bill to, to really be, be picking favorites. And I think it should apply universally, uh, certainly to, um, there are people who are working in and from, um, Singapore, for example, the uk.

Um, but our, our general proposition is that rather than just focus on, on one jurisdiction and, and perhaps, uh, give you the preference, the the rule should apply where possible broadly. And that'll allow people to make decisions about where they want to run their businesses that aren't, uh, tax driven.

Okay. Thank you. Thanks, chair. Okay. Thank you. Any other questions, members? No, I think we'll leave it there. So, um, thank you, um, Peter Wilson and Julie Fright, um, from N-Z-I-E-R on that submission. Thank you, Mr. Chairman and members. Thank, thank you for your time. Okay.

Uh, Mr. Clark, have we got, uh, Katrina Evans from the, uh, privacy Foundation nz? Uh, is she in the, in the waiting room? Yes, she's there now. Thank you. Okay. Uh, good morning Ms. Evans. We look forward to your submission. Thank you for the, um, for your written, Um, good morning to the committee.

Thanks very much for the opportunity to talk to you. Um, as our submission notes, we are focused on, um, so jump jumping within the bill for you on clause 1, 4 3, um, which is the information sharing clause, the proposed new agreements between ministers. Um, as we said in our submission, um, the Privacy Foundation supports responsible information sharing across government.

I've personally been involved, um, as a, as a privacy advisor in government for many, many years, and have been, uh, have been sort of deeply involved in information sharing agreements. It's, it's essential that we share information across agencies where that's appropriate. Privacy's not about keeping things secret, it's not about keeping things off limits.

It's about ensuring that people's, uh, information is properly respected and protected. And in the government context where people have no choice but to provide their information, uh, very often I don't, I don't have a choice about whether I provide my information to inline revenue, nor should I. Um, it's about maintaining trustworthy relationships with people, uh, as a, as sort of, I'm obviously not gonna read out the submission, but, uh, the house recently considered obviously the regulatory standards bill, and I wonder whether there were some pal, uh,

now the Regulatory Standards Act, I wonder whether there were some parallels here that might be helpful, regardless of what, whether one agrees with the contents of the bill or not. And I know there were different opinions, you know, within the house on that it's common ground that it's really important to have good laws and good regulation. Um, there are two aspects of that that are particularly relevant, we think, to this clause.

The first is it shouldn't double up what you already have. And as we pointed out in our submission, there's already a regime under the Privacy Act, the what's called the AA regime that approved information sharing agreements to support public services. You, it can, uh, cater for two way shares, which this clause doesn't do.

It only talks about disclosures by and inland revenue. What if an agency wants to receive information from inland revenue? It can also, the asa regime can also include non-government agencies as parties, as long as it's government agency in the lead. So it's very flexible. Um, also for good law, you need to have a really clear purpose for what you're doing, and the content of that law needs to be adequate for that purpose.

And our submission points out various ways in which, um, it is our view that these, these, this clause 1, 4 3 does not go far enough, uh, particularly no public consultation, uh, no opportunity for people to say if information about them is incorrect.

Um, that's the, your, uh, before, before action is taken against them. We don't wanna be in a robo jet situation, right. People have to have an opportunity to, to comment, comment and various other points. Reporting is, is not adequately catered for by the bill. Um, and with that, I'd like to pass the committee for any questions that you might have.

Th thank, thank you. I'm gonna go to, uh, Nui Walters. Uh, thank you Mr. Chair. And thank you very much for your submission. I don't know if you heard the Law Society submission earlier, but they were raising similar issues. Right. And we're going to go in and have a look at whether it's possible to look at the language of this bill. I tend to agree with you that the better model is, uh, the information sharing agreements, which look to me to be, um, a far better process and indeed exclusion of the Privacy Act provisions

where there are exclusions are done by order and counsel, aren't they within that process? Yes. Fantastic. Um, and the, I did have a question around IPP three A, and it's the application to this bill if it's passes as is. Um, so obviously there's a concern about whether individuals are notified. Would they still, even if it's not in the language of this bill, they would still be required to be notified under I IPP three A? Wouldn't they?

That would be my understanding, yes. Okay, fantastic. And one more, Mr. Chair, if that's, um, please. Alright. In terms of an agency, so say police customs or the SFO making a request for the collection of information, is that provided for an existing information sharing agreements?

Uh, it can be. So for example, one of the, as we pointed out in our submission, uh, one of the reasons for that, uh, ministers can enter into agreements is for, uh, you know, enforcement for, you know, serious crime, for example. And there are existing ACEs that allow for that. So there's little reason why if those don't cover it, why you couldn't adjust them to bring in extra parties or, you know, o otherwise sort of tweak their contents to fit, for example, a new technology environment.

You may want to do things in slightly different ways. We want to take advantage of that. There's no reason why you can't develop and amend those. And there's already the inland revenue MSD agreement as well for protection of public revenue, uh, through, through benefits. So, yeah. Thank you. And am I correct that that's not the case in terms that is the case or it's not the case in terms of the language of this bill?

So being able to, those agencies being able to request the Connection, the agencies can't, under this provision request information as we read it, it talks about disclosure, disclosure, disclosure from a non-revenue. Okay. Thank you. That's useful. Thank you. Ms. Evans. Could you just give us a, just to better understand this, a a hypothetical example of where a, uh, privacy breach could be materially harmful, uh, when information is, uh, shared across government agencies, not just a privacy breach, but where is materially harmful, uh, to, to New Zealanders?

So you, can you give a plot, give us a, paint us an example of where that could, where that could happen and what it would look like? So one of the main things you have to watch out for, I've been, as I said, I've been personally involved with developing these kind of agreements, is, um, whether the systems operate, whether the systems in each agency actually operate together well.

Um, and you can get inaccuracy of information, um, oh, sorry. Inaccuracies with the information could crop up if your, if your match criteria are not sufficiently, um, accurate, for example, and that might mean that, um, inland revenue or the receiving agency could get information that is not accurate enough for its purpose.

It has to spend a lot of time, it has to spend a lot of resources in sorting out those, those accuracy issues. Um, and, and sort of checking the data, um, rechecking the data, possibly changing the data, um, that is, it is inefficient. And so one of the things that you always have to think about is, is how accurate is it?

Um, as far as harming New Zealand goes, um, I, I suggest that if we had a situation such as robo debt in New Zealand, um, then that has a significant impact, not only on the privacy of the individuals, but also on the country as a whole. Excuse me.

Okay. We'll, we'll leave it at that. Thank, thank you for appearing before us, uh, uh, Katrina Evans, uh, chair of the Privacy Foundation nz and thank you for your written submission as well. You're welcome. Thank you very much For today. Thank you. Okay. Uh, Liam, have we got rewiring out here? Rowa? Are they queued up?

Uh, no, not yet. We're about 10 minutes ahead of time, so I don't think we're here yet. Okay. Okay. Well, should we, um, should we, let's just, uh, we, we we're due for a little bit of a break. Let's take a 10 minute break to 10 45, 8 minutes. Uh, and we'll be back ready to roll then. That's the break for the morning. Thank you. Thanks, Mr. Chair.
Okay.

Right. Members? It's 10 45. Sorry, Mr. Chair. We still don't have rewiring here. Okay. We weren't able to get in touch with 'em. We did give them a call and they didn't respond, so don't know if they'll turn up. Okay. Um, have we got anyone else queued up? No. Barbara Gilchrist isn't due till 11, so, Okay. Well, could we go in and, um, could we bring that item forward?

Who have we got as far as members go? It's a non-controversial item. We've got Deborah here, haven't we? Um, Well, sorry, I'm talking about if you're gonna do Yeah, Yeah, yeah. I, I know that, um, I'm just saying, should we, if with the Scott Simpson Amendment paper and letter, um, should we, uh, should we talk about that in private now and then that clears the decks and then come back, uh, well and truly ahead of 11?

Yep, we can do that. Okay. So I just Wanna move across to the private. Yeah. Yep. So me members, we're going to item five, which was scheduled for hub past 12. It's, uh, hopefully a non-controversial, um, item. Uh, so we'll see you in private. Thank you.
Are we in the private or the public here? Cameron?
I
Are you good to get call for? Excellent. Thank you. What's that?

Okay. Right. Uh, have we did rewiring out here? Rowa? Uh, are they on queue there? Let's have a look. Uh, no, we didn't get them in the end, but we do have, uh, Kathleen Ryan, who's here, not Barbara Gilchrist. Okay. Uh, let's bring in Ms. Ryan. Ms. Ryan.

Okay. I can see you. Um, Kathleen, welcome. We are now live streaming. We are, we are back on to the taxation bill, uh, hearings. Um, you have got an individual submission here that we've, uh, take, we can take as read, and we look forward to your five minute presentation. Thank you very much. I'm an older Kiwi who was brought up in a country where the gap between rich and poor was about five and a half times.

And that meant that we were a very easy society to live in because everybody had their basic needs met. I'm concerned that this bill undermines re well fairness and equity in the tax system by limiting or actually denying IRD, the ability to research the tax of high net worth individuals and to look at the kind of tax that they pay and the kind of tax that the poor people pay.

For example, when labor change section and it reduced 17 gb, it enabled IRD to look at the amount that high net worth individuals paid in tax, they pay around about 9% on average compared with about 20, 20% for poor people. Now, that situation has since been compounded by 68% of the tax cuts going to the top 40% of income earners.

And it's no wonder our society has been dragging along because if you give more of the tax cuts to lower and middle income, new Zealanders without much money, they will spend it because their pent up demand is quite great, and that helps turn the wheel, the wheels of the economy.

Whereas for richer people, what do they do? Well, they pay down their mortgages or they invest. So I want to see section 17 GB retained.
